Let $\ph$ be a sentence in which the only connectives are $\neg$, $\conj$, and
$\disj$. The _dual_ of $\ph$ is the sentence which results from replacing every
occurrence of the connective $\wedge$ in $\ph$ with the connective $\vee$, and
vice versa. For example, the dual of the sentence:

$$ (P\conj Q)\vee R $$

is the sentence:

$$ (P\disj Q)\conj R$$

If two sentences $\ph$ and $\psi$ are logically equivalent,
then so are their duals. Illustrate this by confirming (using truth tables) that the following
equivalences are still true when the sentences are replaced with their duals:

a. $\neg(P\conj Q)\equiv \neg P\disj\neg Q$

b. $P\conj(Q\vee R)\equiv (P\conj Q)\vee (P\conj R)$

A sentence is _self-dual_ if it is logically equivalent to its own dual.

c. Show that the sentence:

    $$ (P\conj Q)\vee(Q\conj R)\vee (P\conj R) $$

    is self-dual. Describe what this sentence means in way that explains why
    it is self-dual.
